title: Understanding ML Model Poisoning: How It Happens and How to Detect It
gist: AI-security researcher Igor Maljkovic argues data poisoning is a real, growing threat to ML pipelines (not theoretical), surveys attack techniques (label flipping, backdoors, clean-label, DoS, gradient manipulation), real incidents (Tay, Google Image Search), and prescribes layered defenses combining detection tools like IBM's ART with traditional security controls.

themes.theme: Poisoning as a real, growing threat

insights.claim: Data poisoning is a real and growing threat that can stealthily undermine ML models by introducing maliciously crafted training examples whose consequences sometimes surface only months or years after deployment.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: Frames ML data integrity as a latent, deferred risk — material for assessing AI-deployment risk and demand for AI-security tooling.
insights.quote: Data poisoning is a real and growing threat that can stealthily undermine machine learning models

insights.claim: As organizations increasingly build on public or crowdsourced datasets, the risk of data poisoning grows, because attacker tweaks become hidden landmines shaping model behavior with consequences revealed long after deployment.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: Reliance on open/crowdsourced training data — the norm for many AI builders — is the structural attack surface; a due-diligence flag for AI supply chains.
insights.quote: as organizations increasingly build on public or crowdsourced datasets, the risk of data poisoning grows

insights.claim: Backdoor attacks inject training samples containing a specific trigger (pattern, watermark, artifact) so the model learns to associate the hidden trigger with an attacker-chosen output while performing normally on benign data, letting anyone with knowledge of the trigger reliably manipulate predictions at inference.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: Backdoors evade standard accuracy testing, making them a stealthy supply-chain risk for any deployed classifier (e.g., facial recognition, malware detection).
insights.quote: The model learns to associate this hidden trigger with a particular output chosen by the attacker.
insights.locator: Backdoor attacks are another major threat

insights.claim: Clean-label poisoning injects correctly labeled but adversarially perturbed examples that appear benign to humans and pass standard data-quality checks, using feature-space collision so poisoned samples overlap target instances and cause targeted misclassification at inference.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: Because clean-label attacks bypass label and quality checks, they are the hardest class to detect — driving demand for representation-space/behavioral defenses.
insights.quote: the injected samples appear benign to human observers and pass standard data quality checks, making them difficult to detect
insights.locator: Clean-label poisoning attacks have gained significant attention

insights.claim: Additional poisoning techniques include label flipping (intentional mislabeling to degrade accuracy), outlier injection (extreme samples that push decision boundaries), Denial-of-Service poisoning (corrupted samples that destabilize or break the model), and gradient manipulation attacks that influence the learning process itself.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: A taxonomy of attack vectors that defenders must collectively anticipate; no single defense covers all.
insights.locator: Common Techniques for Poisoning ML Models

insights.claim: Microsoft's chatbot Tay is a real example of online data poisoning, where malicious users exploited its continuous learning from Twitter to feed harmful prompts, causing it to rapidly generate offensive and racist statements — a fate shared by chatbots BabyQ, XiaoBing, and Lee Luda.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: Even big-tech systems have been poisoned in production; concrete evidence that online-learning AI products carry reputational/operational risk.
insights.quote: malicious users exploited Tay's ability to learn continuously from its environment by feeding it harmful prompts
insights.locator: Real-World Examples of Data Poisoning Attacks

insights.claim: A Google Image Search poisoning attack saw a group upload mislabeled images so that searches returned manipulated results, and Google's automated content validation failed to detect the sophisticated attack — showing automated validation alone is insufficient.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: Demonstrates automated validation at hyperscaler scale can be defeated, underscoring the need for layered human + ML defenses.
insights.quote: Google's automated content validation systems failed to detect the attack due to its sophistication
insights.locator: Another notable example is a Google Image Search

insights.claim: High-value target domains for poisoning include spam filters (mislabeled not-spam emails), medical ML systems (insiders injecting mislabeled cancerous-as-benign images causing delayed diagnoses), and antivirus ML models (malware labeled benign creating detection blind spots), with even small proportions of poisoned data causing severe consequences.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: Maps where poisoning has the highest real-world stakes (health, security) — guides where defensive investment matters most.
insights.locator: Common Target Domains for Data Poisoning Attacks

insights.claim: Detection requires layering multiple techniques — statistical signals (label-distribution drift, feature-space irregularities), representation-space analysis, spectral/rank-based methods, influence-based auditing, and provenance/cross-dataset checks — because the most effective attacks are crafted to be indistinguishable from clean samples and defeat traditional sanity checks.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: No single detector suffices; the layered, adaptive requirement defines the product surface for ML-security vendors.
insights.quote: your detection mechanisms must be as creative and adaptive as the adversaries you're up against

insights.claim: IBM's open-source Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART) is presented as the most comprehensive open-source poisoning-detection toolkit (activation clustering, spectral signature analysis, outlier detection, supporting TensorFlow/Keras/PyTorch/scikit-learn/XGBoost), but remains research-oriented and may lack the robustness required for large-scale production.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: Open-source tooling exists but the production gap signals a commercial opportunity for specialized AI-security companies.
insights.quote: there is no magic bullet for data poisoning detection

insights.claim: Recommended defenses combine traditional security controls (RBAC, data access policies, encryption) with ML-specific controls — data validation (TFDV, Great Expectations), integrity/provenance tracking (checksums, digital signatures, DVC, LakeFS), training/production data separation, canary samples and golden datasets, and robust training that limits malicious-sample influence.
insights.speaker: Igor Maljkovic
insights.so_what: A concrete defense-in-depth checklist that maps the emerging MLOps-security tooling category and named tools to track.
insights.locator: Techniques for Securing ML Training Pipelines
